content: In Isaiah 9:6, 'For to us a child is born.' What does the Hebrew word for 'child' mean?
explanation: Yeled (ילד) means 'child' — referring to Jesus born as an infant.
options: Bachur — young man
Zaken — old man
Yeled — child
Naar — youth

content: In Isaiah 53:5, 'He was wounded for our transgressions.' What does the Hebrew for 'wounded' mean?
explanation: Chalal (חלל) means 'pierced through, wounded' — pointing to the death of Christ on the cross.
options: Aven — sin
Ra — evil
Naga — to touch
Chalal — pierced through, wounded

content: Isaiah 1:18: 'Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be ___'
explanation: The LORD promises full forgiveness, transforming sins as red as blood into white as snow.
options: White as snow
Like new beginnings
Clean as water
Beautiful as flowers

content: What did the seraphim cry to one another in Isaiah's vision?
explanation: Isaiah 6:3 — 'Holy' (Kadosh) is repeated three times, emphasizing God's absolute holiness.
options: Glory to God
Hallelujah
Amen
Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts

content: Isaiah 11:6 describes the peaceful kingdom: 'The wolf shall dwell with ___'
explanation: Isaiah 11:6 — A vision of the peaceful age when Messiah reigns, all hostility is reconciled.
options: The goat
The lion
The bear
The lamb

content: Isaiah 40:8: 'The grass withers, the flower fades, but the ___ of our God will stand forever'
explanation: Everything on earth is temporary, but God's Word is eternal. 1 Peter 1:24-25 quotes this verse.
options: Temple
Word
Love
Covenant

content: How does Isaiah 55:8-9 compare God's thoughts and ways to man's?
explanation: God's thoughts are higher than man's as the heavens are higher than the earth. We cannot fully comprehend Him.
options: The same
Slightly different
Cannot be compared
As high above as the heavens are above the earth

content: Isaiah 64:8 compares God and us to what?
explanation: God is the potter, we are the clay. He has the right to shape us as He wills.
options: Potter and clay
Father and son
Teacher and student
King and people

content: When the LORD asked, 'Whom shall I send?' how did Isaiah reply?
explanation: Isaiah 6:8 — 'Here am I! Send me.' Isaiah was ready to obey after being cleansed.
options: I am unworthy
Here am I, send me
Send another
Let me think about it

content: Isaiah 7:14 prophesies: 'Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name ___'
explanation: Isaiah 7:14 — Immanuel (God with us). This prophecy is fulfilled in Jesus (Matt 1:23).
options: Jesus
Michael
Immanuel
Messiah

content: By what names does Isaiah 9:6 call the coming Messiah?
explanation: Isaiah 9:6 — Four titles showing Jesus is both God and man, both King and Priest.
options: King of Kings
Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace
Son of Man
Lamb of God

content: What does Isaiah 40:31 say will happen to those who wait for the LORD?
explanation: Isaiah 40:31 — Those who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles, run and not be weary, walk and not faint.
options: Power
Wealth
Renew strength, mount up with wings like eagles
Knowledge

content: To what animal does Isaiah 53:6 compare humanity?
explanation: Isaiah 53:6 — All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned every one to his own way. But the LORD has laid on Him (Christ) the iniquity of us all.
options: Birds flying away
Fish in a stream
Cattle wandering
Sheep gone astray

content: Isaiah 53:7 describes the Suffering One 'like a lamb that is led to the slaughter.' To what event does this image point?
explanation: Isaiah 53:7 — Jesus stood silent before Pilate (Matt 27:12-14), like a lamb led to slaughter. Philip used this passage to preach the gospel to the eunuch (Acts 8:32-35).
options: Jesus silent before Pilate, then crucified
The captivity of Israel
The Passover
The daily sacrifice

content: Whom does Isaiah 14:12 describe as fallen from heaven like the morning star?
explanation: Isaiah 14:12 — 'How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of Dawn! How you are cut down to the ground, you who laid the nations low!' Traditionally understood as Lucifer/Satan.
options: A good angel
King David
The Day Star (Lucifer) — applied to Satan
King Solomon

content: Whom does Isaiah 26:3 say God keeps in perfect peace?
explanation: Isaiah 26:3 — 'You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you.'
options: The one whose mind is stayed on Him
The wealthy
Those bringing many sacrifices
The most righteous

content: What does Isaiah 41:10 promise to God's people?
explanation: Isaiah 41:10 — 'Fear not, for I am with you; be not dismayed, for I am your God; I will strengthen you, I will help you.'
options: Will grant wealth
Fear not — I am with you and will strengthen you
Will give many descendants
Will destroy all enemies

content: What does the LORD do with His people's sins, according to Isaiah 43:25?
explanation: Isaiah 43:25 — 'I, I am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins.'
options: Remember them forever
Punish severely
Blot them out and remember them no more
Pass them on to the descendants

content: Whom does Isaiah 61:1-2 describe as anointed to bring good news to the poor?
explanation: Isaiah 61:1-2 — 'The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me, because the LORD has anointed me to bring good news to the poor.' Jesus applied this to Himself (Luke 4:18-19).
options: The prophet Elijah
King David
Samuel
The Messiah (Jesus quoted it of Himself)

content: What does Isaiah 65:17 promise God will create?
explanation: Isaiah 65:17 — 'For behold, I create new heavens and a new earth, and the former things shall not be remembered or come into mind.'
options: A new temple
A new king
A new army
New heavens and a new earth

content: According to Isaiah 1:1, Isaiah son of whom received this vision, during which kings of Judah?
explanation: Isaiah 1:1 — 'The vision of Isaiah son of Amoz, which he saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah'. Isaiah ministered through 4 kings (~740-686 BC), about 60 years. NOT Amos the prophet (a different book).
options: Son of Amos — during Solomon
Son of Jesse — during David
Son of AMOZ — during UZZIAH, JOTHAM, AHAZ, and HEZEKIAH kings of Judah
Son of Samuel — during Saul

content: According to Isaiah 2:4, in the latter days, the nations will RECAST weapons into what?
explanation: Isaiah 2:4 — 'They shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore'. The famous verse — inscribed on the UN building in New York; describing absolute peace in the Messianic kingdom (cf. Mic 4:3 same verse).
options: Recast into swords + spears (as described in the passage)
RECAST SWORDS into PLOWSHARES, SPEARS into PRUNING HOOKS — no nation will lift sword against another, nor train for WAR anymore
Recast into gold coins (according to the text's framing)
Recast into idols (per the textual narrative)

content: According to Isaiah 6:1, Isaiah's calling vision occurred in what year?
explanation: Isaiah 6:1 — 'In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord seated on a throne, high and exalted, and the train of his robe filled the temple'. King Uzziah (Azariah) died ~740 BC of leprosy for offering incense unlawfully (2 Chr 26:16-21). Isaiah ch 6 = his TRUE call narrative (chapters 1-5 prefigure the content).
options: The year Ahab died (per the textual narrative)
The year David ascended (according to the text's framing)
The year Solomon built the temple
THE YEAR KING UZZIAH DIED — Isaiah saw the Lord sitting on a throne, high + lofty

content: According to Isaiah 11:1, the Messiah is pictured as what emerging from the stump of Jesse (David's father)?
explanation: Isaiah 11:1 — 'A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit'. 'Branch' (netzer) became the basis for 'Nazarene' = Christ (Mt 2:23). Meaning: though David's line appears 'cut down' (Babylonian exile), a SHOOT will spring up = Messiah. Rev 22:16 quotes 'I am the Root and Descendant of David'.
options: A SHOOT — 'a SHOOT will COME UP from the STUMP of JESSE; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit'
A newborn child (as described in the passage)
An angel (according to the text's framing)
A lamb (per the textual narrative)

content: According to Isaiah 11:2, the Spirit of the Lord rests on the Messiah with 7 characteristics. Besides 'the Spirit of the Lord', 6 more: 'Spirit of wisdom + understanding, Spirit of counsel + might, Spirit of knowledge + ___'?
explanation: Isaiah 11:2 — 'The Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and might, the Spirit of knowledge and the fear of the Lord'. 6 attributes + the Spirit of the Lord himself = 7 (= the 7 Spirits in Rev 1:4, 4:5). Structure 3 pairs: (1) wisdom + understanding; (2) counsel + might; (3) knowledge + fear of the Lord.
options: Spirit of miracles
Spirit of FEAR of the LORD
Spirit of courage
Spirit of creation

content: According to Isaiah 25:8, what will God do on the MOUNTAIN of salvation (= Mt Zion)?
explanation: Isaiah 25:8 — 'He will swallow up death forever. The Sovereign Lord will wipe away the tears from all faces; he will remove his people's disgrace from all the earth'. This verse is QUOTED in Rev 21:4 (the new creation) + 1 Cor 15:54 (resurrection). 'SWALLOW UP DEATH' = total reversal.
options: Provide abundant food (as described in the passage)
Wage war (per the textual narrative)
SWALLOW UP DEATH FOREVER + WIPE TEARS from all faces + remove his people's disgrace from all the earth
Build a wall (according to the text's framing)

content: According to Isaiah 40:3, a voice calls in the wilderness: '___ the way of the LORD; MAKE STRAIGHT in the desert a highway for our God'?
explanation: Isaiah 40:3 — 'A voice cries: In the wilderness prepare the way of the Lord; make straight in the desert a highway for our God'. All 4 Gospels apply this to John the Baptist — the messenger preparing Christ's way. Opens part 2 of Isaiah (40-66) characterized by the 'comfort message'.
options: Avoid (according to the text's framing)
Burn (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Destroy (as described in the passage)
PREPARE — this verse is QUOTED in all 4 Gospels for John the Baptist (Mt 3:3, Mk 1:3, Lk 3:4, Jn 1:23)

content: According to Isaiah 40:1-2, what is the opening of Isaiah's second part?
explanation: Isaiah 40:1-2 — 'Comfort, comfort my people, says your God. Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and proclaim to her that her warfare is ended, that her iniquity is pardoned, that she has received from the Lord's hand double for all her sins'. Part 1 (1-39) = JUDGMENT theme; Part 2 (40-66) = COMFORT + REDEMPTION theme. 40:1 = the famous transition (also the inspiration for Handel's 'Messiah').
options: 'COMFORT, COMFORT my PEOPLE, says your God. Speak tenderly to JERUSALEM, and proclaim her HARDSHIP is OVER'
'Repent' (per the textual narrative)
'God hates sin' (according to the text's framing)
'Keep the Sabbath' (as described in the passage)

content: According to Isaiah 42:1, how does the FIRST SERVANT SONG open?
explanation: Isaiah 42:1 — 'Behold my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights; I have put my Spirit upon him; he will bring forth justice to the nations'. THIS is the FIRST of 4 SERVANT SONGS (42:1-4, 49:1-6, 50:4-9, 52:13-53:12). The Father QUOTES this at Christ's baptism (Mt 3:17 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased').
options: 'Hear my words' (per the textual narrative)
'The LORD has chosen Israel' (as described in the passage)
'BEHOLD MY SERVANT, whom I UPHOLD; my CHOSEN, in whom my SOUL DELIGHTS. I have put my SPIRIT upon him'
'Satan has fallen' (according to the text's framing)

content: According to Isaiah 45:1, which foreign king does the LORD call his 'ANOINTED' (messiah) — who would conquer Babylon + free the Jews?
explanation: Isaiah 45:1 — 'Thus says the Lord to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him'. Isaiah PROPHESIES king CYRUS by NAME ~150 years before Cyrus was born. Fulfillment: Cyrus conquered Babylon ~539 BC, issued the decree freeing the Jews (Ezra 1).
options: Darius (per the textual narrative)
CYRUS — king of Persia
Nebuchadnezzar (as described in the passage)
Artaxerxes (according to the text's framing)

content: According to Isaiah 53:3, how is the suffering Servant described?
explanation: Isaiah 53:3 — 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief; and as one from whom men hide their faces he was despised, and we esteemed him not'. Prophesied ~700 BC describing Christ's suffering. 'Despised' appears twice — ABSOLUTE rejection.
options: A mighty king (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
A righteous man (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
A child (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
A man DESPISED + REJECTED by men; a man of SORROWS, acquainted with GRIEF; one from whom men hide their FACES, despised, and we esteemed him NOT

content: According to Isaiah 53:11, what is the outcome of the suffering Servant?
explanation: Isaiah 53:11 — 'Out of the anguish of his soul he shall see and be satisfied; by his knowledge shall the righteous one, my servant, make many to be accounted righteous, and he shall bear their iniquities'. JUSTIFICATION OF MANY = the reason for the suffering. A core Protestant verse (Paul cites it in Rom 5:18-19).
options: 'He will SEE the FRUIT of his SUFFERING and be SATISFIED. By his KNOWLEDGE my righteous SERVANT will JUSTIFY MANY'
'He will die and vanish' (according to the text's framing)
'He will sleep in the grave' (as described in the passage)
'He will fight' (per the textual narrative)

content: According to Isaiah 55:1, what is the invitation to the THIRSTY?
explanation: Isaiah 55:1 — 'Come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters; and you who have no money, come, buy and eat! Come, buy wine and milk without money and without cost'. The FREE invitation — incompatible with 'salvation by works'. Source of inspiration for Rev 22:17 ('Whoever is thirsty, come...without price').
options: 'Go seek gold' (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
'Buy much food' (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
'COME, ALL WHO are THIRSTY, COME to the WATERS! And you who have NO MONEY, come, BUY and EAT! Come, BUY WINE + MILK WITHOUT MONEY, WITHOUT PRICE'
'Be rich' (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)

content: According to Isaiah 55:11, how is God's word described?
explanation: Isaiah 55:10-11 — 'As the rain and the snow come down from heaven, and do not return there but water the earth, making it bring forth and sprout, giving seed to the sower and bread to the eater, so shall my word be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose, and shall succeed in the thing for which I sent it'. God's word SELF-FULFILLING (effective) — theological basis for Sola Scriptura.
options: A temporary word (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
The word that GOES OUT from his mouth WILL NOT RETURN to him EMPTY; it will ACCOMPLISH what he desires + ACHIEVE the purpose for which he SENT it
A word only for angels (according to the text's framing)
A word only about Israel (as described in the passage)

content: According to Isaiah 58:6-7, what is the FASTING God really wants?
explanation: Isaiah 58:6-7 — 'Is not this the fast that I choose: to loose the bonds of wickedness, to undo the straps of the yoke, to let the oppressed go free, and to break every yoke? Is it not to share your bread with the hungry and bring the homeless poor into your house; when you see the naked, to cover him?' God prioritizes SOCIAL JUSTICE over religious ritual (cf. Amos 5).
options: 40-day fast (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Wearing sackcloth (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
Bowing the knee (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
LOOSE the chains of injustice, BREAK the YOKE of oppression, set the OPPRESSED FREE; SHARE bread with the HUNGRY, bring the HOMELESS POOR into your house, CLOTHE the NAKED

content: According to Isaiah 60:1, what is the call to Zion (Jerusalem)?
explanation: Isaiah 60:1 — 'Arise, shine, for your light has come, and the glory of the Lord has risen upon you'. Chapters 60-62 = the high point of 'salvation to Zion'. This verse = source of many hymns + Rev 21:23 ('the glory of God gives it light').
options: 'ARISE, SHINE; for your LIGHT has come, and the GLORY of the LORD has RISEN upon you'
'Mourn' (according to the text's framing)
'Flee' (per the textual narrative)
'Surrender' (as described in the passage)

content: According to Isaiah 53:4, what does the suffering Servant BEAR for us?
explanation: Isaiah 53:4 — 'Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows; yet we esteemed him stricken, smitten by God, and afflicted'. Mt 8:17 quotes this applied to Christ's healing ministry. The FIRST role of REPLACEMENT (substitution) in the Servant Song of ch 53.
options: Only small sins (according to the text's framing)
BORE our INFIRMITIES + CARRIED our SORROWS — yet we esteemed him STRICKEN, SMITTEN by God, and AFFLICTED
Only shame (per the textual narrative)
Only difficulties (as described in the passage)

content: According to Isaiah 66:1-2, what does the Lord say about his HOUSE and place of REST?
explanation: Isaiah 66:1-2 — 'This is what the Lord says: Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool. Where is the house you will build for me? Where will my resting place be? Has not my hand made all these things?' Stephen QUOTES this in Acts 7:49-50 before the Sanhedrin.
options: 'Build me a great temple' (according to the text's framing)
'Solomon's temple is the only one' (as described in the passage)
'HEAVEN is my THRONE + EARTH is my FOOTSTOOL — what kind of HOUSE will you build for me? Has not my HAND made all these things?'
'Build city walls' (per the textual narrative)

content: According to Isaiah 53:12, what is the climactic outcome of the Servant?
explanation: Isaiah 53:12 — 'Therefore I will divide him a portion with the many, and he shall divide the spoil with the strong, because he poured out his soul to death and was numbered with the transgressors; yet he bore the sin of many, and makes intercession for the transgressors'. Final verse of the Song. Lk 22:37 quotes 'numbered with transgressors' applied to Christ crucified between 2 robbers.
options: He will be king on earth (as described in the passage)
He will teach (according to the text's framing)
He will heal (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
He POURED OUT his SOUL TO DEATH, was NUMBERED with the TRANSGRESSORS; he BORE the SIN of MANY + INTERCEDES for the transgressors

content: According to Isaiah 1:18 (already in Easy), the Lord INVITES the people to REASON. What follows shows about FORGIVENESS?
explanation: Isaiah 1:18 — 'Come now, let us reason together, says the Lord: though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow; though they are red like crimson, they shall become like wool'. The FORMULA of forgiveness: from deep red → pure white = by GRACE, NOT works. Scarlet + crimson = colors of blood / heavy sin.
options: Cannot forgive (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Forgive only the doers of good (according to the text's framing)
'Though your sins are like SCARLET, they shall be as WHITE AS SNOW; though they are RED LIKE CRIMSON, they shall become like WOOL'
Forgive only those who sacrifice (as described in the passage)

content: According to Isaiah 5:1-7 (Song of the Vineyard), the VINEYARD = who? What did the owner expect?
explanation: Isaiah 5:7 — 'For the vineyard of the LORD of hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah are his pleasant planting; and he looked for justice, but behold, bloodshed; for righteousness, but behold, an outcry!' Hebrew wordplay: mishpat (justice) vs mispach (bloodshed); tsedaqah (righteousness) vs tse'aqah (cry). Christ BUILDS on this parable in Mt 21:33-44.
options: = Cyrus / glory (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
= the HOUSE of ISRAEL, men of Judah = the cherished planting; the owner EXPECTED sweet GRAPES (= justice + righteousness) but got WILD grapes
= Philistia / victory (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
= Egypt / milk + honey (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)

content: According to Isaiah 6:5, when seeing the vision of the Lord on the throne + seraphim crying 'Holy 3 times', what was Isaiah's FIRST reaction?
explanation: Isaiah 6:5 — 'And I said: Woe is me! For I am lost; for I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips; for my eyes have seen the King, the LORD of hosts!' The right reaction to ENCOUNTERING the holy God = recognition of one's SIN. Then a seraph touches a coal to his lips → 'your guilt is taken away' (6:7). Pattern: see → confess → cleansed → sent.
options: 'I will proclaim' (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
'I am willing' (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
'I rejoice' (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
'WOE IS ME! For I am LOST! For I am a man of UNCLEAN LIPS, dwelling among a people of UNCLEAN LIPS; for my eyes have seen the KING, the LORD of hosts!'

content: According to Isaiah 6:9-10, what is the PARADOXICAL message in Isaiah's specific call?
explanation: Isaiah 6:9-10 — 'And he said, Go, and say to this people: Keep on hearing, but do not understand; keep on seeing, but do not perceive. Make the heart of this people dull, and their ears heavy, and blind their eyes; lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their hearts, and turn and be healed'. This is the FAMOUS prophecy of HARDENING — the people miss their CHANCE to repent. QUOTED 6 times in the NT (Mt 13:14, Mk 4:12, Lk 8:10, Jn 12:40, Acts 28:26-27, Rom 11:8) — always applied to the Jews' rejection of Christ.
options: The people will repent immediately (as described in the passage) (per the original content)
'Go and tell this people: You will be ever HEARING, but never UNDERSTANDING; ever SEEING, but never PERCEIVING — make the HEART of this people CALLOUSED' (= judgment of hardening)
They will rejoice (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence) (cross-referenced with related passages)
They will rise to battle (according to the text's framing) (as the text indicates)

content: According to Isaiah 7:14, what sign did the Lord give Ahaz in the THREAT of Syria + Israel?
explanation: Isaiah 7:14 — 'Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign: The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel'. Mt 1:22-23 QUOTES this applied to Christ. Two-level fulfillment: (1) short-term = Isaiah/Ahaz's son; (2) ultimate = Christ via MARY. Immanuel = God WITH US = Christ is God incarnate.
options: Fire from heaven (according to the text's framing)
A new star (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
A VIRGIN (almah) will CONCEIVE + give BIRTH to a SON, and will call him IMMANUEL ('God with us')
An angel rescuer (as described in the passage)

content: According to Isaiah 9:6, what FOUR names of the Messiah are foretold?
explanation: Isaiah 9:6 — 'For to us a child is born, to us a son is given; and the government shall be upon his shoulder, and his name shall be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace'. The famous verse — source of inspiration for Handel's 'Messiah' + many Christmas hymns.
options: 'WONDERFUL COUNSELOR, MIGHTY GOD, EVERLASTING FATHER, PRINCE OF PEACE'
'King, Priest, Prophet, Judge'
'Redeemer, Forgiver, Comforter, Peace'
'Life, Way, Truth, Glory' (per the textual narrative)

content: According to Isaiah 11:6-9, in the Messianic kingdom, how do ANIMALS coexist?
explanation: Isaiah 11:6-9 — 'The wolf shall dwell with the lamb, and the leopard shall lie down with the young goat, and the calf and the lion and the fattened calf together; and a little child shall lead them... The nursing child shall play over the hole of the cobra, and the weaned child shall put his hand on the adder's den. They shall not hurt or destroy in all my holy mountain'. Imagery = REVERSING the curse of Genesis 3 — cosmic harmony in the Messianic kingdom.
options: Still carnivorous (as described in the passage) (per the original content) (per the textual narrative)
Extinct (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence) (cross-referenced with related passages)
Only sleep (according to the text's framing) (as the text indicates) (in the context of the passage)
WOLVES with LAMBS; LEOPARDS with KIDS; CALVES + lions + fattened cattle EAT TOGETHER, a LITTLE CHILD leads them; cows + bears graze; infants play with snakes — NONE will harm on the holy mountain

content: According to Isaiah 14:12-15, how is the king of Babylon's PRIDE described — applied to SATAN's fall?
explanation: Isaiah 14:12-14 — 'How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of Dawn! How you are cut down to the ground, you who laid the nations low! You said in your heart, I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne on high; I will sit on the mount of assembly in the far reaches of the north; I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High'. 'Day Star' (Lucifer in the Latin Vulgate) → became the name 'Satan' in tradition. 5 'I WILLs' = 5 ambitions.
options: 'How you have FALLEN from heaven, MORNING STAR, son of the dawn!... You said in your heart, I will ASCEND to heaven; I will RAISE my throne ABOVE the stars of God'
'I am the king of Israel' (as described in the passage) (per the original content)
'I am righteous' (according to the text's framing) (as the text indicates)
'I love God' (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)

content: According to Isaiah 26:19, what is the EARLIEST OT prophecy of bodily RESURRECTION?
explanation: Isaiah 26:19 — 'Your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise. You who dwell in the dust, awake and sing for joy! For your dew is a dew of light, and the earth will give birth to the dead'. This + Daniel 12:2 = the 2 clearest OT passages on BODILY resurrection (most of the OT is vague about Sheol/Hades). The Protestant doctrine of BODILY RESURRECTION is core teaching.
options: 'Souls return to God' (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
'The dead are gone' (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
'YOUR DEAD will LIVE; their BODIES will RISE. You who dwell in the dust, AWAKE and shout for joy! Your DEW is like the dew of MORNING; the earth will give birth to her dead'
'God saves only souls' (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)

content: According to Isaiah 35:5-6, what are the signs of the Messianic kingdom? (QUOTED by Christ + John the Baptist)
explanation: Isaiah 35:5-6 — 'Then the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf unstopped; then shall the lame man leap like a deer, and the tongue of the mute sing for joy. For waters break forth in the wilderness, and streams in the desert'. Christ QUOTES this when John the Baptist (in prison) asks, 'Are you the one who is to come?' (Mt 11:5, Lk 7:22). Christ replies by LISTING the miracles he had done = exactly the content of Isaiah 35.
options: The sun shines brightly (as described in the passage)
Stars fall (according to the text's framing) (as the text indicates)
Much rain (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
EYES of the BLIND will be OPENED, EARS of the DEAF unstopped; the LAME will LEAP like a deer, TONGUES of the MUTE will sing for JOY

content: According to Isaiah 40:11, how does God care for his people — a tender image?
explanation: Isaiah 40:11 — 'He will tend his flock like a shepherd; he will gather the lambs in his arms; he will carry them in his bosom, and gently lead those that are with young'. SHEPHERD imagery — Ezk 34, Jn 10, 1 Pet 5:4 all use it. Christ calls himself the 'Good Shepherd'. This verse is GENTLER than Isaiah 40:10 'The Lord comes with might + his arm rules' — strength + tenderness coexisting.
options: 'Like a SHEPHERD he will TEND his flock; he gathers the LAMBS in his ARMS and CARRIES them close to his HEART; he gently leads those that have YOUNG'

The structure intentionally mirrors OT/NT.

content: TYPE 3 deep — Isaiah 6:3 'Holy, holy, holy' (TRISHAGION) is unique in the OT how? What's the meaning of 3-fold repetition?
explanation: Isaiah 6:3 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts!' (Hebrew: qadosh, qadosh, qadosh) = TRISHAGION (Greek: 'thrice-holy'). Hebrew feature: NO comparative/superlative → REPETITION elevates. 1 repeat = holy; 2 = very holy; 3 = ABSOLUTELY HOLY (super-superlative). This is the ONLY OT instance where a divine attribute is tripled. Rev 4:8 echoes this — the 4 living creatures also cry 'Holy 3x'. SOURCE of inspiration for the 'Sanctus' in church liturgy. Some theologians see a hint at the TRINITY but this is secondary.

content: TYPE 3 deep — Why do many modern scholars call Isaiah 40-66 'DEUTERO-ISAIAH' / 'TRITO-ISAIAH'? The CONSERVATIVE Protestant view?
explanation: Scholarly debate: (1) MODERN VIEW (since Duhm 1892): Isaiah divided into 3 — Proto-Isaiah (1-39, 8th BC), Deutero-Isaiah (40-55, ~550 BC, Babylonian exile), Trito-Isaiah (56-66, post-return). Reason: part 2 mentions Babylon + Cyrus by SPECIFIC NAME; different style. Premise: humans CANNOT know specific futures → must be multiple authors at different times. (2) TRADITIONAL PROTESTANT VIEW: Isaiah 1 wrote ALL ~8th BC; God knows the future + reveals it through the prophet; Mt 12:17-18 quotes Isa 42 + Acts 8:32-35 quotes Isa 53 — the NT calls the AUTHOR 'Isaiah' (not multiple). Christ + the early church accepted 1 author. NO ancient evidence for multiple authors; the Deutero theory is SPECULATION based on philosophical presupposition (anti-supernatural).

content: TYPE 3 deep — Servant Song 4 (Isa 52:13-53:12) has what SPECIAL POETIC structure?
explanation: Isaiah 52:13-53:12 structure: 5 strophes × 3 verses = 15 verses. CHIASTIC structure (symmetrical): - A (52:13-15) God's voice: 'Behold, MY servant' — Servant exalted; - B (53:1-3) Community's voice 'WE' did not believe; describes Servant's suffering; - C CENTER (53:4-6) Community recognizes SUBSTITUTION — 'He bore in our place'; - B' (53:7-9) More on suffering + burial; - A' (53:10-12) God's voice: 'My servant will see his offspring'. The CENTRAL pillar = 53:4-6 'he bore in our place' = the MAIN message. Symmetrical structure = the most refined Hebrew poetic technique. This is one of the OT's most highly-crafted poems.
options: FIVE STROPHES x 3 verses = 15 verses; opening (52:13-15) + closing (53:11b-12) = God's voice ('MY Servant'); 3 middle strophes (53:1-3, 4-6, 7-9, 10-11a) = COMMUNITY's voice ('WE'); CHIASM = pattern A-B-C-B'-A' with the central verse being SUBSTITUTION (53:4-6)
